description: Highly contagious viral illness causing severe hemorrhagic gastroenteritis and myocarditis in puppies. The virus attacks rapidly dividing cells in the intestinal tract, bone marrow, and heart, leading to severe dehydration, immunosuppression, and potentially death within 48-72 hours of clinical onset if untreated.
common_causes: Direct contact with infected dog feces
Fomite transmission via contaminated surfaces, bowls, shoes
risk_factors: Certain breeds: Rottweilers, Dobermans, Pit Bulls
home_care: Isolate the dog from other animals immediately
Provide fresh water if dog is not vomiting
Clean all contaminated areas with 1:30 bleach solution
Wash hands thoroughly after contact
Follow strict quarantine protocol for 4 weeks
warning_signs: Bloody or black tarry diarrhea
Persistent vomiting leading to inability to keep water down
Severe lethargy with unresponsiveness
Rapid onset of dehydration (skin tenting, dry gums)
Hypothermia (temperature below 99F)
Seizures or collapse
when_to_visit_vet: Immediately at first suspicion. Prognosis worsens significantly with every hour of delay. This is a life-threatening emergency requiring intensive veterinary care including hospitalization and IV fluid therapy.
prevention: Core vaccination beginning at 6-8 weeks with boosters every 3-4 weeks until 16 weeks
Annual booster vaccination as recommended by veterinarian
Limit exposure to unvaccinated dogs and high-traffic dog areas until fully vaccinated
Avoid dog parks, pet stores, and boarding during vaccination period
Quarantine new puppies for 2 weeks before introduction
affected_age: Primarily puppies 6-20 weeks; older unvaccinated dogs also susceptible
recovery_time: 5-14 days with intensive care; survivors have lifelong immunity
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Canine Parvovirus
American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA): Parvovirus FAQs
Journal of Veterinary Internal Medicine: CPV-2 epidemiology

description: A serious multisystemic viral disease affecting the respiratory, gastrointestinal, and nervous systems of dogs. Caused by a paramyxovirus closely related to the measles virus. The disease follows a biphasic fever pattern and can cause permanent neurological damage in survivors.
common_causes: Direct contact with infected dogs or wildlife
Fomite contamination of food bowls and bedding
Transplacental transmission from dam to puppy
symptoms: Biphasic fever (temperature spike, improvement, then second spike)
Loss of appetite and weight loss
risk_factors: Exposure to wildlife reservoirs (raccoons, foxes, skunks)
home_care: Strict isolation from other animals
Warm, quiet, and draft-free environment
Soft food if able to eat, assisted feeding if needed
Gently clean nose and eyes of discharge
Monitor temperature and neurological status hourly
Provide padded bedding if seizures occur
warning_signs: Seizures or neurological deterioration
Complete loss of coordination
Difficulty breathing from pneumonia
Collapse or unresponsiveness
Signs of secondary bacterial infection
when_to_visit_vet: At the first sign of symptoms. Early supportive care significantly improves survival odds. Any dog with fever, respiratory signs, and neurological signs requires immediate hospitalization.
prevention: Core DHP/DHPP vaccination beginning at 6-8 weeks
Booster vaccinations every 3-4 weeks until 16 weeks of age
Avoid contact with wildlife and unvaccinated dogs
Disinfect premises with appropriate virucidal agents
Quarantine new dogs before introducing to household
recommended_tests: RT-PCR on conjunctival or nasal swabs
affected_age: Most common in puppies 3-6 months; can affect unvaccinated dogs of any age
recovery_time: 2-6 weeks for acute phase; neurological deficits may be permanent
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Canine Distemper
AVMA: Distemper in Dogs
Journal of Comparative Pathology: CDV pathogenesis

description: A highly contagious respiratory syndrome characterized by inflammation of the trachea and bronchi. Multiple pathogens can be involved, most commonly Bordetella bronchiseptica bacteria in combination with canine parainfluenza virus or canine adenovirus type 2.
symptoms: Persistent dry hacking cough often described as goose-honk
Gagging or retching at end of cough
risk_factors: Crowded living conditions with poor ventilation
home_care: Rest and limit exercise for 7-10 days
Use a harness instead of a collar to reduce tracheal pressure
Humidifier or steam therapy to soothe airways
Avoid smoke, dust, and strong fragrances
Separate from other dogs for 2 weeks
warning_signs: Difficulty breathing or open-mouth breathing
Lethargy with complete loss of appetite
Fever persisting beyond 48 hours
Cough producing thick yellow-green discharge
Signs of progressing to pneumonia
when_to_visit_vet: If cough persists beyond 7 days, if fever develops, if the dog stops eating, or if the cough worsens. Puppies and elderly dogs should be seen sooner.
prevention: Bordetella vaccination (intranasal or injectable)
Avoid high-density dog environments during outbreaks
Good ventilation and sanitation in kennels
Reduce stress during boarding
Allow 2 weeks between vaccination and potential exposure
recommended_tests: Complete blood count to assess inflammation
affected_age: All ages; more severe in puppies and geriatric dogs
breed_risk: Brachycephalic breeds (pugs, bulldogs) may have more severe symptoms
recovery_time: 7-21 days; cough may persist 2-3 weeks after other symptoms resolve
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Canine Infectious Respiratory Disease Complex
Journal of the American Veterinary Medical Association: Bordetella management
WSAVA guidelines for canine respiratory vaccination

description: A fatal viral encephalomyelitis caused by a lyssavirus that affects all mammals. It is the deadliest zoonotic disease with near-100% fatality once clinical signs appear. The virus travels from the bite wound along peripheral nerves to the central nervous system.
common_causes: Bite from an infected animal (wildlife or domestic)
Saliva from an infected animal contacting open wounds or mucous membranes
Raccoons, skunks, foxes, and bats are primary wildlife reservoirs
Inhalation of aerosolized virus in bat caves (rare)
symptoms: Prodromal phase: behavioral changes, anxiety, unusual friendliness or aggression
Furious form: hyperexcitability, aggression, roaming, disorientation
Paralytic form: progressive paralysis starting from hind legs
Excessive drooling and foaming at the mouth
risk_factors: Living in regions with high wildlife rabies prevalence
home_care: No home care is possible or safe
Do not approach the animal if rabies is suspected
Contact local animal control immediately
If bitten, wash wound thoroughly with soap and water for 15 minutes
Seek immediate post-exposure prophylaxis at a hospital
Report case to local health department
warning_signs: Any unexplained behavioral change in a previously normal dog
Sudden aggression or unusual tameness
Progressive paralysis
Excessive drooling without other cause
Loss of fear of humans or other animals
when_to_visit_vet: Any suspected rabies exposure requires immediate veterinary consultation and local health department notification. If a dog shows neurological signs or has bitten someone, it must be quarantined or tested per local regulations.
prevention: Mandatory rabies vaccination by 12-16 weeks of age
Booster vaccination at 1 year and every 1-3 years depending on local laws and vaccine type
Avoid free-roaming to reduce wildlife contact
Report stray or unusual behaving animals to authorities
Post-exposure prophylaxis for any bite wounds
recommended_tests: Direct fluorescent antibody test on brain tissue (post-mortem)
Serology for antibody titer in vaccinated animals
affected_age: All ages; risk increases with age if vaccination is not maintained
recovery_time: No recovery; nearly 100% fatal once clinical signs appear. Incubation period: 2 weeks to 6 months.
references: World Health Organization: Rabies fact sheet
CDC: Rabies in dogs
MSD Veterinary Manual: Rabies
Compendium of Animal Rabies Prevention and Control

description: A serious and potentially fatal parasitic disease caused by foot-long worms (Dirofilaria immitis) that live in the heart, pulmonary arteries, and adjacent blood vessels. Transmitted through mosquito bites, the worms cause progressive damage to the heart and lungs over months to years.
common_causes: Travel to or relocation from endemic regions
symptoms: Exercise intolerance and fatigue after minimal activity
Swollen abdomen due to fluid accumulation (right heart failure)
Caval syndrome: sudden collapse with dark urine
risk_factors: Geographic location (endemic areas include tropical and subtropical regions)
Hunting or working dogs with prolonged outdoor exposure
Previous history of heartworm in household
home_care: Strict exercise restriction to prevent pulmonary thromboembolism
Comfortable environment away from heat and exertion
Balanced nutrition to maintain body condition
Monitor breathing rate and effort daily
Administer prevention as directed year-round
warning_signs: Sudden collapse or fainting
Coughing up blood
Severe difficulty breathing
Distended abdomen that feels tight
Blue-tinged gums
Inability to rise or walk
when_to_visit_vet: Annual testing is essential in endemic areas. If a dog shows any respiratory signs or exercise intolerance and is not on prevention, test immediately. Emergency if caval syndrome is suspected.
prevention: Year-round heartworm prevention medication prescribed by veterinarian
Annual antigen and microfilaria testing as recommended
Minimize mosquito exposure during dawn and dusk
Remove standing water sources
recommended_tests: Heartworm antigen test (ELISA): detects adult female worms
Chest radiographs for pulmonary artery changes
Echocardiography for visible adult worms in advanced cases
affected_age: Most commonly diagnosed in dogs aged 2-8 years; risk increases with cumulative mosquito exposure
recovery_time: Slow kill protocol: 6-12 months. Adulticide treatment: 2-4 months with strict exercise restriction during treatment
references: American Heartworm Society: Canine heartworm guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Heartworm Disease
Journal of Veterinary Internal Medicine: Heartworm treatment protocols

description: A bacterial zoonotic disease caused by pathogenic Leptospira species. The bacteria infect the kidneys and liver, causing acute organ failure. It is transmitted through contact with urine-contaminated water, soil, or food of infected animals, particularly rodents and wildlife.
common_causes: Exposure to infected wildlife (rats, raccoons, opossums)
Swimming in or drinking from contaminated ponds or puddles
symptoms: Yellowing of eyes, gums, and skin (jaundice)
Reduced or absent urine production (kidney failure)
risk_factors: Access to ponds, lakes, or standing water
Rural or suburban environment with wildlife
Hunting or working dogs with water exposure
Rodent infestation in home or kennel
home_care: Handle with gloves as bacteria shed in urine
Isolate from other pets and family members
Provide fresh water if kidney function is monitored
Encourage urination away from living areas
Disinfect urine-contaminated surfaces with bleach solution
Follow antibiotic course exactly as prescribed
warning_signs: Absence of urine output for 12+ hours
Severe jaundice (yellow discoloration)
Bleeding from nose or gums
Seizures or disorientation
Collapse or inability to stand
when_to_visit_vet: Any combination of fever, lethargy, and increased thirst requires urgent evaluation. If jaundice is visible or urine output decreases, treat as an emergency.
prevention: Leptospirosis vaccination (non-core but recommended for at-risk dogs)
Eliminate standing water sources
Rodent control in and around the property
Prevent drinking from ponds and puddles during walks
Handle and dispose of dog urine with care
recommended_tests: PCR testing on blood or urine
Complete blood count and blood chemistry panel
affected_age: All ages; higher risk in young adult dogs (1-3 years) with outdoor exposure
recovery_time: 2-4 weeks with prompt treatment; lifelong kidney damage may persist in severe cases
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Leptospirosis
CDC: Leptospirosis in dogs and humans
Journal of Veterinary Internal Medicine: Canine leptospirosis consensus

description: Inflammation of the stomach and intestinal lining causing acute onset vomiting and diarrhea. Usually self-limiting and caused by dietary indiscretion but can be triggered by infections, parasites, or dietary changes.
common_causes: Dietary indiscretion (eating garbage, spoiled food, foreign objects)
symptoms: Mild to moderate loss of appetite
risk_factors: Recently adopted dogs with diet change
home_care: Withhold food for 12-24 hours to rest the digestive tract
Provide small amounts of fresh water frequently
Introduce bland diet (boiled rice with boiled chicken or pumpkin)
Feed small frequent meals once vomiting stops
Gradually transition back to regular food over 3-4 days
Probiotics to restore gut flora
warning_signs: Vomiting blood or coffee-ground material
Bloody or black tarry diarrhea
Persistent vomiting beyond 24 hours
Complete inability to keep water down
Severe lethargy or collapse
Abdominal pain with vocalization
when_to_visit_vet: If vomiting persists beyond 24 hours, if blood is present, if the dog cannot keep water down, or if lethargy is severe. Puppies and senior dogs should be seen at 12 hours of persistent symptoms.
prevention: Keep garbage and compost inaccessible
Make dietary changes gradually over 5-7 days
Avoid table scraps and high-fat foods
Use slow feeder bowls to prevent gulping
Maintain consistent feeding schedule
recommended_tests: Complete blood count to assess hydration and infection
Abdominal radiographs if foreign body is suspected
affected_age: All ages; more common in dogs under 2 years due to exploratory eating habits
breed_risk: Labradors and Golden Retrievers (higher rates of dietary indiscretion)
recovery_time: 24-72 hours with proper supportive care
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Acute Gastroenteritis in Small Animals
Journal of Small Animal Practice: Dietary management of gastroenteritis

description: A developmental orthopedic condition where the hip joint develops abnormally due to laxity of the joint capsule and malformation of the acetabulum, leading to subluxation, arthritis, and pain. It has a strong genetic component but is influenced by environmental factors.
common_causes: Abnormal joint laxity in early development
Rapid growth rate in large breed puppies
Excessive exercise on hard surfaces during growth
symptoms: Hind limb lameness worsening after exercise
Reluctance to climb stairs or jump
Swaying of the hips when walking
Pain on hip extension or rotation
home_care: Maintain lean body weight to reduce joint stress
Low-impact exercise (swimming, leash walks)
Provide orthopedic memory foam bedding
Use ramps for vehicles and stairs
Joint supplements containing glucosamine and chondroitin
Warmth therapy for stiff joints
Physical therapy and massage
warning_signs: Complete inability to stand or walk
Crying out in pain when touched
Severe muscle wasting over hindquarters
Dragging of hind limbs
when_to_visit_vet: If a puppy shows lameness or gait abnormality, seek evaluation for early intervention. Adult dogs with progressive lameness should be examined to manage arthritis before it becomes debilitating.
prevention: Responsible breeding with OFA/PennHIP certification
Maintain lean body condition throughout life
Controlled exercise on soft surfaces during growth
Balanced nutrition without excess calcium or calories
Avoid high-impact activity until skeletal maturity
recommended_tests: OFA (Orthopedic Foundation for Animals) hip screening
Radiographic evaluation under sedation for joint laxity
Physical examination with Ortolani and Barden maneuvers
affected_age: Signs may appear as early as 4-12 months; degenerative changes progress with age
recovery_time: Lifelong management required; surgical recovery 8-12 weeks; medical management ongoing
references: Orthopedic Foundation for Animals (OFA): Hip Dysplasia database
MSD Veterinary Manual: Hip Dysplasia
Journal of the American Veterinary Medical Association: CHD management

description: A highly contagious viral disease caused by feline parvovirus, characterized by severe leukopenia, gastroenteritis, and high mortality in kittens. The virus attacks rapidly dividing cells in the bone marrow, intestinal tract, and developing brain.
common_causes: Direct contact with infected cats or their bodily fluids
Fomite transmission via food bowls, bedding, litter boxes
Indoor contamination as the virus survives months on surfaces
Transplacental transmission causing cerebellar hypoplasia in kittens
risk_factors: Stress from weaning, relocation, or surgery
home_care: Strict isolation from other cats for at least 2 weeks
Do not attempt home treatment, requires intensive veterinary care
Warmth support with heating pads (low setting, wrapped)
Gentle cleaning of soiled fur and bedding
Bleach-clean all surfaces the cat contacted
Hand hygiene before and after contact
warning_signs: Bloody or watery diarrhea leading to rapid dehydration
Persistent vomiting preventing fluid intake
Rapid deterioration with severe lethargy
Low body temperature (hypothermia)
Collapse or unresponsiveness
Sudden death in young kittens
when_to_visit_vet: Immediately at the first sign of fever, vomiting, or lethargy. This is a medical emergency requiring hospitalization, IV fluids, and intensive care.
prevention: Core FVRCP vaccination beginning at 6-8 weeks
Booster vaccinations every 3-4 weeks until 16-20 weeks
Annual booster as recommended
Keep indoor-only lifestyle
Disinfect environment with bleach solution (1:32)
Quarantine new cats for 2 weeks
recommended_tests: Fecal ELISA for feline parvovirus antigen
Complete blood count (severe leukopenia is hallmark)
affected_age: Primarily kittens 2-6 months; unvaccinated cats of any age susceptible
recovery_time: 5-14 days with intensive hospital care; survivors have lifelong immunity
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Feline Panleukopenia
American Association of Feline Practitioners: FPV guidelines
Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery: FPV management

description: A highly contagious respiratory infection primarily caused by feline herpesvirus type 1 (FHV-1) and feline calicivirus (FCV). These viruses cause acute inflammation of the nasal passages, sinuses, eyes, and throat, with herpesvirus establishing lifelong latency with periodic reactivation.
common_causes: Feline herpesvirus type 1 (FHV-1) - majority of cases
Direct contact with infected cats or contaminated surfaces
symptoms: Oral ulcers on tongue and palate (FCV)
risk_factors: Stress from boarding, travel, or new pets
home_care: Gently clean eyes and nose with warm damp cloth several times daily
Offer strongly aromatic warmed wet food to stimulate appetite
Run a humidifier or bring cat into steamy bathroom
Reduce stress with quiet environment
Keep food and water bowls within easy reach
Ensure hydration with wet food or syringe water
warning_signs: Difficulty breathing with open-mouth breathing
Refusing all food for 24+ hours
Severe lethargy and unresponsiveness
Yellow-green or bloody nasal discharge
Ulceration of the cornea (eye cloudiness)
Dehydration signs (skin tenting)
when_to_visit_vet: If the cat stops eating or drinking for more than 24 hours, shows difficulty breathing, develops eye ulcers, or if fever persists beyond 3 days despite home care.
prevention: FVRCP vaccination (core vaccine)
Reduce stress in multi-cat households
Good ventilation and avoid overcrowding
Quarantine new cats for 2 weeks before introduction
Use separate food bowls and litter boxes
recommended_tests: PCR panel for FHV-1, FCV, and secondary pathogens
Fluorescein stain to check for corneal ulcers
affected_age: All ages; kittens and elderly cats most severely affected
breed_risk: Brachycephalic breeds (Persians, Himalayans) more prone to severe symptoms
recovery_time: 7-21 days for acute infection; herpesvirus remains latent for life with periodic reactivation
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Feline Respiratory Infections
American Association of Feline Practitioners: URI guidelines
Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery: FHV-1 and FCV management

description: A retrovirus that causes immunosuppression, anemia, and lymphoma in infected cats. FeLV is one of the most important infectious diseases of cats, significantly shortening lifespan and predisposing to secondary infections and neoplasia.
common_causes: Prolonged close contact with infected cats
Sharing food bowls and water sources
Transplacental transmission from queen to kittens
symptoms: Progressive weight loss and muscle wasting
risk_factors: Multi-cat households with unknown FeLV status
Aggressive cats that fight with others
Shelter cats with unknown vaccination and exposure history
home_care: Keep the cat strictly indoors to prevent spread and protect from infections
Provide high-quality nutrition to support immune function
Minimize stress with predictable routine and quiet environment
Monitor for signs of secondary infection
Regular dental care to prevent stomatitis
Monthly weigh-ins to track weight loss
warning_signs: Persistent fever unresponsive to supportive care
Severe weight loss despite good appetite
Difficulty breathing with open-mouth breathing
Sudden onset of pale or yellow gums
Seizures or neurological signs
Bleeding from nose, mouth, or in stool
when_to_visit_vet: Any FeLV-positive cat showing new symptoms should be evaluated promptly. Routine checkups every 6 months are recommended for FeLV-positive cats.
prevention: FeLV vaccination (recommended for at-risk cats)
Keep cats indoors to avoid contact with infected strays
Test all new cats before introducing to household
Separate FeLV-positive cats from negative cats
Routine annual FeLV testing for at-risk cats
recommended_tests: ELISA test for FeLV p27 antigen (screening)
IFA test for confirmation (bone marrow detection)
Complete blood count to assess anemia and cell counts
affected_age: Most commonly diagnosed in young to middle-aged cats (1-6 years); kittens most susceptible
recovery_time: Lifelong infection with progressive disease; median survival 2-4 years after diagnosis
references: American Association of Feline Practitioners: FeLV guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Feline Leukemia Virus
Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery: FeLV management

description: A lentivirus that progressively compromises the immune system of cats, making them vulnerable to secondary infections. FIV is species-specific and transmitted primarily through deep bite wounds during territorial fighting.
common_causes: Deep bite wounds from infected cats
symptoms: Weight loss and poor coat condition
risk_factors: Intact male cats (most likely to fight)
History of bite abscesses or wounds
home_care: Strictly indoor-only to reduce exposure to pathogens
High-quality, balanced diet to support immune function
Regular dental care and monitoring for oral disease
Stress reduction with environmental enrichment
Monthly weight monitoring
Flea and parasite prevention to reduce disease burden
warning_signs: Persistent fever lasting more than 48 hours
Oral ulcers or severe dental disease causing drooling
Chronic diarrhea leading to weight loss
Neurological signs such as circling or seizures
Recurrent infections not responding to treatment
when_to_visit_vet: Any cat with recurrent infections, chronic oral disease, or unexplained weight loss should be tested for FIV. Routine biannual wellness exams are recommended for confirmed positive cats.
prevention: Keep cats indoors exclusively
Neuter to reduce fighting behavior
Test all new cats before introduction to household
Separate FIV-positive from FIV-negative cats
No effective vaccine currently available in most regions
affected_age: Most commonly diagnosed in middle-aged to older cats (5-10 years)
recovery_time: Lifelong infection; many FIV-positive cats live normal lives with proper care for years
references: American Association of Feline Practitioners: FIV guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Feline Immunodeficiency Virus
Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery: FIV management

description: A complex syndrome encompassing multiple conditions affecting the feline bladder and urethra. The most common form is feline idiopathic cystitis, a sterile inflammation of the bladder that shares features with human interstitial cystitis.
common_causes: Feline idiopathic cystitis (most common, 55-65% of cases)
Calcium oxalate or struvite uroliths (bladder stones)
Urethral plugs composed of matrix and crystals
Bacterial urinary tract infection (uncommon in young cats)
symptoms: Straining to urinate with prolonged squatting
Excessive licking of the genital area
risk_factors: Dry food only diet with low moisture intake
Male cats (higher risk of urethral obstruction)
home_care: Increase water intake with wet food diet and water fountains
Reduce stress with environmental enrichment
Provide multiple clean litter boxes (one per cat plus one)
Use unscented clumping litter
Feliway pheromone diffusers to reduce stress
Interactive play sessions daily
Maintain stable daily routine
warning_signs: Complete inability to urinate (urethral obstruction - emergency)
Vocalizing in pain and straining with no urine output
Vomiting and lethargy with large painful bladder
Collapse or unresponsiveness
Blood in urine with obstruction signs
when_to_visit_vet: Any sign of urinary obstruction (straining without urine production) is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate veterinary care. Non-obstructive cases should be seen within 24 hours.
prevention: Feed high-moisture diet (canned or raw)
Maintain healthy body weight
Minimize environmental stress with predictable routine
Provide vertical space, hiding spots, and perches
Multiple clean litter boxes in quiet locations
Regular veterinary checkups with urinalysis
recommended_tests: Abdominal radiography or ultrasound for uroliths
affected_age: All ages; most common in middle-aged cats (2-7 years)
recovery_time: 3-14 days for acute episode; lifelong management required for chronic cases
references: American Association of Feline Practitioners: FLUTD guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease
Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery: FIC consensus statement

description: A progressive, irreversible decline in kidney function commonly affecting older cats. The kidneys gradually lose their ability to filter waste, concentrate urine, and regulate electrolytes. Most cases are due to chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis.
symptoms: Increased thirst and urination (compensatory early sign)
risk_factors: Previous acute kidney injury or toxin exposure
home_care: Feed prescription renal diet with restricted protein and phosphorus
Ensure constant access to fresh, clean water at multiple stations
Provide water fountains to encourage drinking
Administer subcutaneous fluids as directed by veterinarian
Maintain consistent feeding schedule with small frequent meals
Use low-stress handling and environment
warning_signs: Complete loss of appetite for more than 24 hours
Severe vomiting preventing medication or food intake
Oral ulcers on tongue or gums
Seizures or muscle tremors
Sudden weakness or collapse
Breath smelling of ammonia or urine
when_to_visit_vet: At first diagnosis for staging and treatment planning. Any cat with CKD showing anorexia, vomiting, or lethargy needs immediate evaluation for potential uremic crisis.
prevention: Annual senior wellness blood work starting at age 7
High-moisture diet throughout life
Regular dental care to prevent periodontal disease
Avoid nephrotoxic medications and monitor drug doses
Prompt treatment of urinary tract infections
Maintain good hydration
recommended_tests: Blood chemistry with BUN and creatinine
affected_age: Most common in cats over 7 years; 30-50% of cats over 15 years affected
recovery_time: Chronic progressive condition; management lifelong; survival varies from months to years depending on stage at diagnosis
references: International Renal Interest Society (IRIS): CKD staging guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Feline Chronic Kidney Disease
Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery: CKD management consensus

description: A common endocrine disorder where the body cannot properly produce or utilize insulin, leading to hyperglycemia. Type II diabetes (insulin resistance) is most common in cats, often associated with obesity. With proper management, many cats achieve remission.
symptoms: Weight loss despite increased or normal appetite
home_care: Administer insulin injections exactly as prescribed, at same times daily
Monitor water intake and urination patterns daily
Weekly weight checks
Provide a quiet, stress-free environment
Ensure fresh water is always available
Keep a log of appetite, thirst, and behavior changes
warning_signs: Sudden weakness, collapse, or seizures (hypoglycemia from insulin overdose)
Vomiting, lethargy, and dehydration with sweet-smelling breath (ketoacidosis)
Complete loss of appetite for more than 12 hours
Extreme thirst and urination (polyuria/polydipsia)
Walking on hocks (plantigrade stance)
when_to_visit_vet: Any cat with increased thirst, weight loss, and increased urination should be tested. Emergency if ketoacidosis is suspected: unresponsive, vomiting, or breathing difficulty.
prevention: Feed a low-carbohydrate, high-protein prescription diabetic diet consistently
Consistent feeding schedule with 2-3 meals at fixed times daily
Monitor water intake and urination patterns daily
Weekly weight checks
Regular veterinary checkups for blood glucose monitoring
recommended_tests: Urinalysis with glucose and ketone testing
Serial blood glucose curves (during insulin adjustment)
Thyroid panel to rule out concurrent hyperthyroidism
affected_age: Most commonly diagnosed in middle-aged to older cats (7-12 years)
recovery_time: Lifelong management; diabetic remission possible in 30-50% of cats with early diagnosis and weight management
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Feline Diabetes Mellitus
American Association of Feline Practitioners: Diabetes guidelines
Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery: feline diabetes management

description: Inflammation of the mammary gland parenchyma, almost always caused by bacterial infection. It is the most economically significant disease of dairy cattle worldwide, leading to reduced milk yield, discarded milk, early culling, and increased treatment costs.
symptoms: Abnormal milk with clots, flakes, or watery appearance
Swollen, hot, and painful udder quarter
Reduced milk yield in affected quarter
Systemic signs: fever, depression, loss of appetite
Dehydration and rapid breathing in severe cases
Chronic cases: fibrosis and atrophy of udder quarter
risk_factors: Introduction of infected cows to herd
home_care: Hygienic milking: clean teats thoroughly before and after milking
Use teat dip post-milking (veterinary grade)
Separate cows with clinical mastitis from the herd
Discard all abnormal milk
Maintain clean, dry, well-bedded stalls
Ensure proper milking machine function and maintenance
warning_signs: Gangrene of the udder (cold, blue-black skin)
Systemic illness with high fever and collapse
Complete loss of milk production in quarter
Signs of septicemia
Recurrence in the same quarter after treatment
when_to_visit_vet: Severe cases with systemic signs (fever, depression) require immediate veterinary attention. Mild cases not responding to standard therapy within 24-48 hours also need veterinary evaluation.
prevention: Good milking hygiene: clean dry teats, teat dipping, backflushing
Regular milking machine maintenance and testing
Hygienic and dry bedding materials
Dry cow therapy at drying off
Culling of chronic carriers
Biosecurity for new herd additions
Proper milking technique and complete milk-out
recommended_tests: Milk culture and sensitivity for pathogen identification
affected_age: Adult lactating cows; highest risk in early lactation and periparturient period
recovery_time: 3-14 days depending on severity; chronic cases may never recover full production
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Bovine Mastitis
National Mastitis Council: recommended mastitis control program
Journal of Dairy Science: mastitis epidemiology

description: A highly contagious viral vesicular disease affecting cloven-hoofed animals. Caused by an aphthovirus of the Picornaviridae family, FMD is the most economically devastating livestock disease globally.
common_causes: Foot and Mouth Disease Virus (FMDV): seven serotypes (O, A, C, SAT1, SAT2, SAT3, Asia1)
symptoms: Vesicles (blisters) on the tongue, lips, and oral mucosa
Excessive drooling and foaming at the mouth
Vesicles on the coronary band and interdigital space of feet
Severe lameness with reluctance to move
Weight loss and reduced feed intake
Abortions and infertility in breeding stock
risk_factors: Introduction of new animals without quarantine
Farm visitors and vehicles with prior animal contact
home_care: No home care: this is a reportable disease requiring mandatory government involvement
Immediately isolate affected animals from the herd
Restrict all animal movement on and off the premises
Do not treat animals; await official veterinary instructions
Implement strict biosecurity: footbaths, separate clothing
Report to local veterinary authorities immediately
warning_signs: Sudden lameness affecting multiple animals
Profuse drooling in otherwise healthy-looking cattle
Vesicles or erosions in the mouth or on feet
Sharp milk drop across the herd
Sudden death in young animals (myocarditis)
when_to_visit_vet: Immediate reporting to government veterinary authorities is mandatory by law when FMD is suspected. Do not wait for confirmation before reporting.
prevention: Government-regulated vaccination programs in endemic regions
Strict biosecurity protocols on farm entry and exit
Quarantine for 28 days for all new animal introductions
Import restrictions from affected regions
Controlled slaughter and disposal in outbreaks
Movement restrictions during outbreaks
Regular surveillance and serological monitoring
affected_age: All ages; severe disease in young animals with mortality from myocarditis
recovery_time: 2-6 months for full recovery; chronic production losses often persist
references: World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH/OIE): FMD technical guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Foot and Mouth Disease
FAO: FMD prevention and control

description: A multifactorial disease complex resulting from an interaction between environmental stressors, viral infections, and bacterial pathogens. It is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in feedlot cattle.
common_causes: Stress from weaning, transport, and commingling
symptoms: Depression and isolation from the group
Reduced feed intake and weight loss
Extended neck breathing in severe cases
risk_factors: Recent transport or shipping of cattle
Weaning stress within the past 30 days
Commingling of cattle from multiple sources
home_care: Isolate affected animals from the group
Provide clean, dry, well-ventilated shelter
Ensure easy access to fresh water and high-quality feed
Minimize dust and ammonia levels in housing
Monitor body temperature and respiratory rate daily
warning_signs: Severe difficulty breathing with open mouth
Complete refusal to eat for 24+ hours
Blue-tinged mucous membranes (cyanosis)
Collapse or inability to stand
High fever not responding to initial treatment
when_to_visit_vet: Any calf or adult with fever and respiratory signs should be examined within 24 hours. Rapid treatment is critical; delays allow progression to irreversible lung damage.
prevention: Vaccination programs targeting viral and bacterial pathogens
Metaphylaxis at arrival in high-risk populations
Stress reduction: proper weaning, short transport, shade
Good ventilation and optimal stocking density
All-in-all-out management in feedlot
Adequate colostrum intake for newborns
recommended_tests: Clinical scoring system for early detection
Complete blood count and acute phase proteins
affected_age: Most severe in young calves and feedlot cattle 6-18 months old
recovery_time: 10-30 days; chronic cases may have permanent lung damage and reduced growth
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Bovine Respiratory Disease Complex
Journal of Veterinary Internal Medicine: BRDC management
University of Nebraska-Lincoln: BRDC prevention guidelines

description: A chronic bacterial disease primarily caused by Mycobacterium bovis, characterized by the formation of granulomatous lesions (tubercles) in the lungs, lymph nodes, and other organs. It is a significant zoonotic disease with human health implications.
common_causes: Inhalation of aerosolized bacteria from infected animals
Direct contact with infected cattle or wildlife reservoirs
Transplacental transmission from dam to calf
symptoms: Progressive weight loss despite adequate appetite
Enlarged superficial lymph nodes (especially head and neck)
risk_factors: Herd with history of TB infection
Large herd size with shared feeding
Purchase of cattle from unknown or infected herds
home_care: No home care is possible or advisable
Report suspected cases to veterinary authorities
Isolate suspect animals from rest of herd
Maintain strict biosecurity
Follow official testing and culling protocols
warning_signs: Persistent cough lasting weeks
Progressive emaciation with good appetite
Visible lymph node swelling
Blood-tinged mucus from coughing
Sudden deterioration with respiratory distress
when_to_visit_vet: Any suspicion of TB requires immediate reporting to government veterinary services. TB is a notifiable disease requiring official testing and control measures.
prevention: Regular herd testing with tuberculin skin test
Culling of positive reactors
Movement control and quarantine of new animals
Biosecurity to prevent contact with wildlife reservoirs
Test and slaughter programs in endemic regions
Pasteurization of milk for human consumption
recommended_tests: Single intradermal comparative cervical tuberculin test (SICCT)
Bacterial culture and PCR for confirmation
affected_age: All ages; chronic course more common in adult cattle
recovery_time: Chronic progressive disease; no treatment, control through test and slaughter
references: World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH): Bovine TB
MSD Veterinary Manual: Bovine Tuberculosis
CDC: Mycobacterium bovis transmission

description: A chronic bacterial disease primarily caused by Brucella abortus, characterized by abortion in the last trimester of pregnancy, retained placenta, and reduced fertility. It is one of the most important zoonotic diseases worldwide.
common_causes: Ingestion of contaminated feed or water
Contact with aborted fetuses, placentas, or fetal fluids
symptoms: Abortion in the last trimester (5-8 months of pregnancy)
Enlarged or inflamed testicles in bulls (orchitis)
Weak calves born at full term
risk_factors: Herd with open or unknown brucellosis status
Introduction of cattle without testing and quarantine
Wildlife reservoirs (bison, elk in some regions)
home_care: Wear gloves when handling aborted fetuses or afterbirth
Bury or incinerate aborted materials
Disinfect contaminated areas with appropriate disinfectant
Isolate aborting cows from the herd
Report cases to veterinary authorities
warning_signs: Multiple abortions in the herd
Abortion storms affecting many cows simultaneously
Retained placentas with foul discharge
Orchitis in bulls
Hygromas with joint swelling
when_to_visit_vet: Any abortion in cattle should be investigated by a veterinarian. Brucellosis is a notifiable disease in most countries, and official testing is mandatory.
prevention: Vaccination of heifer calves (Brucella abortus strain 19 or RB51)
Regular herd serological testing
Quarantine and test all new cattle introductions
Test and slaughter eradication programs
Good hygiene during assisted calving
Avoid bringing in untested breeding stock
recommended_tests: Brucella milk ring test for dairy herds
affected_age: Sexually mature cattle; pregnant cows most severely affected
recovery_time: Chronic infection; no effective treatment; eradication through test and slaughter
references: World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH): Bovine Brucellosis
CDC: Brucellosis
MSD Veterinary Manual: Brucellosis in Cattle

description: A metabolic disorder occurring in early lactation high-producing dairy cows caused by a negative energy balance, leading to excessive fat mobilization and accumulation of ketone bodies. It typically occurs within the first 6-8 weeks of lactation.
common_causes: Negative energy balance in early lactation
High milk production demand exceeding energy intake
symptoms: Neurological signs in severe cases (head pressing, circling, blindness)
risk_factors: Poor transition diet during dry period
Multiple metabolic diseases in early lactation
home_care: Encourage feed intake with high-quality forages and palatable concentrates
Provide fresh, clean water at all times
Ensure comfortable, clean, shaded environment
Propylene glycol drench as directed by veterinarian
Monitor body condition and milk production daily
warning_signs: Complete loss of appetite for more than 24 hours
Neurological signs: head pressing, circling, blindness
Recumbency with inability to stand
Rapid decline in milk production
Acetone breath detectable from a distance
when_to_visit_vet: If a cow shows reduced appetite and declining milk within first 8 weeks of lactation, test for ketosis. Neurological signs or recumbency require emergency veterinary attention.
prevention: Proper transition diet with adequate energy density
Body condition score management: target 3.0-3.5 at calving
Monitor feed intake closely for first 30 days post-calving
Regular ketone testing in early lactation
recommended_tests: Blood chemistry including glucose and liver enzymes
affected_age: Adult lactating cows, primarily 2-8 weeks post-calving
recovery_time: 5-14 days with appropriate treatment and dietary correction
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Ketosis in Cattle
Journal of Dairy Science: transition cow management
Cornell University College of Veterinary Medicine: bovine ketosis

description: A contagious viral disease of water buffalo caused by a parapoxvirus closely related to vaccinia virus. It has zoonotic potential and causes economic losses through reduced milk yield, damage to udders, and hide quality deterioration.
symptoms: Small raised pustular lesions on udder and teats
Tender and painful udder leading to milking difficulty
Fever and systemic signs in severe cases
Lesions on skin of hands of milkers (zoonotic)
home_care: Milk affected animals last and use separate equipment
Gentle handling of the udder during milking
Apply antiseptic ointments as recommended by veterinarian
Improve milking hygiene and teat dipping
Isolate affected animals from healthy herd
warning_signs: Spread of lesions across entire udder
Secondary bacterial mastitis
Severe pain preventing milking
Zoonotic transmission to milkers
when_to_visit_vet: If lesions are severe, spread rapidly, or if secondary mastitis develops. Also if human cases occur among farm workers.
prevention: Good milking hygiene and teat dipping
Separate milking of infected animals
Hand washing and glove use for milkers
Quarantine of newly introduced buffaloes
Disinfection of milking equipment
affected_age: All ages; active milking buffaloes most commonly affected
recovery_time: 2-4 weeks; lesions heal spontaneously in most cases
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Buffalopox
WOAH: Buffalopox technical sheets
Indian Journal of Veterinary Pathology: buffalo pox epidemiology

description: An acute, highly fatal septicemic disease primarily affecting water buffalo and cattle, caused by specific serotypes of Pasteurella multocida. Characterized by high fever, respiratory distress, and sudden death, often within hours of onset.
symptoms: Rapid breathing with extended head and neck
Swelling of the pharyngeal and neck region (edema)
Death within 6-24 hours in peracute cases
risk_factors: Water buffalo breed (higher susceptibility than cattle)
Age: young buffaloes 6 months to 2 years
Seasonal outbreaks: monsoon and humid weather
Endemic regions: South and Southeast Asia, Africa
home_care: No time for home care; immediate veterinary intervention required
Isolate affected animals from the herd
Reduce stress: quiet environment, avoid handling
Provide soft, palatable feed if animal is eating
warning_signs: Sudden death of multiple animals in short period
Visible swelling of the throat and brisket
Severe difficulty breathing with mouth open
High fever unresponsive to basic intervention
Blood-tinged nasal discharge
when_to_visit_vet: Any sudden death in buffaloes or cattle with respiratory signs is a veterinary emergency. Immediate herd-wide intervention is required.
prevention: Vaccination before monsoon season (killed vaccine)
Booster vaccination every 6 months in endemic areas
Avoid overcrowding and ensure good ventilation
Reduce transport stress
Quarantine new animals for 2 weeks
Prompt treatment of exposed animals
recommended_tests: Blood smear for bipolar staining bacteria
Bacterial culture from blood or tissues
affected_age: Young buffaloes 6-24 months most susceptible; all ages at risk
breed_risk: Water buffalo significantly more susceptible than cattle
recovery_time: Peracute death within 6-24 hours; acute cases 3-7 days with intensive treatment
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Hemorrhagic Septicemia
WOAH: Hemorrhagic septicemia guidelines
FAO: HS prevention and control in buffalo

description: A wasting protozoal disease caused by Trypanosoma evansi, transmitted mechanically by biting flies (tabanids, stomoxys). It is one of the most important diseases of buffaloes and camels in Asia, Africa, and South America.
common_causes: Mechanical transmission by tabanid horse flies
Biting fly season and waterlogging conditions
symptoms: Reduced milk yield and reproductive performance
risk_factors: Wet season with high fly population
Waterlogged areas and fly breeding grounds
Endemic regions in Asia and Africa
home_care: Provide high-quality nutrition to support body condition
Stall animals in well-ventilated, fly-proof housing
Use insect repellents on animals
Remove manure and standing water to reduce fly breeding
Rest affected animals from work
warning_signs: Progressive emaciation despite adequate feed intake
Severe anemia with very pale gums
Corneal opacity progressing to blindness
Intermittent fever spikes not responding to general treatment
Recumbency from weakness
when_to_visit_vet: Any buffalo showing chronic weight loss, anemia, and fever in a fly-endemic area should be tested for Surra. Early treatment significantly improves prognosis.
prevention: Vector control: insecticide spraying, fly traps
Housing near fly-proof shelters during peak season
Regular screening in endemic areas
Prompt treatment of positive cases
Single-use needles for all injections
recommended_tests: Blood smear examination for trypanosomes (Giemsa stain)
Wet blood film for motile parasites
ELISA and PCR for chronic low-parasitemia cases
affected_age: All ages; more severe in young and working animals
recovery_time: 2-4 weeks with treatment; chronic cases may require prolonged management
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Trypanosomiasis (Surra)
WOAH: Surra technical disease card
Journal of Veterinary Parasitology: T. evansi in buffalo

description: A highly contagious viral disease of goats and sheep caused by a Morbillivirus closely related to rinderpest and canine distemper. Characterized by severe mucopurulent discharge, pneumonia, diarrhea, and high mortality.
common_causes: Fomite spread via equipment and clothing
symptoms: Serous to mucopurulent nasal and eye discharge
Erosive stomatitis with necrotic oral lesions
Severe pneumonia with coughing and respiratory distress
risk_factors: Introduction of new animals without quarantine
Seasonal outbreaks during dry or cold periods
Mixed grazing with sheep and goats
home_care: Isolate affected animals immediately
Supportive care: soft palatable feed, clean water
Provide warm, dry, and well-ventilated shelter
Gently clean eyes and nose of discharge
Separate sick from healthy animals
Report suspected cases to veterinary services
warning_signs: High fever with profuse nasal and eye discharge
Severe diarrhea leading to rapid dehydration
Respiratory distress with mouth breathing
Oral necrotic lesions
Death within 5-10 days of onset
when_to_visit_vet: Immediately upon suspicion. PPR is a notifiable disease and requires official reporting.
prevention: Annual PPR vaccination (attenuated live vaccine)
Quarantine for 21 days before introducing new animals
Biosecurity: disinfection, separate clothing, footbaths
Avoid mixing goats from different sources
Movement restrictions during outbreaks
recommended_tests: PCR on nasal swabs or blood
affected_age: All ages; young animals 3-12 months most severely affected
recovery_time: 2-4 weeks; mortality rate 50-90% in naive populations
references: WOAH: Peste des Petits Ruminants guidelines
FAO: PPR global eradication program
MSD Veterinary Manual: PPR

description: A persistent lentiviral infection of goats causing chronic arthritis in adults and neurological disease in kids. CAE virus is closely related to the Maedi-Visna virus of sheep, resulting in lifelong carrier status with progressive clinical deterioration.
common_causes: Caprine arthritis encephalitis virus (CAEV, a lentivirus)
Ingestion of colostrum or milk from infected does (kids)
Direct contact: prolonged close association with infected goats
symptoms: Chronic progressive arthritis: swollen carpal (knee) joints
Stiffness, lameness, and reluctance to move
Weight loss and poor body condition despite good appetite
Hard, firm udder (indurative mastitis) reduced milk production
Ataxia: progressive hind limb weakness and paralysis (kids 2-6 months)
Head tilt, circling, and blindness in neurological cases
risk_factors: Kids fed unpasteurized colostrum from infected does
Kids raised on pooled goat milk
Dairy goat herds with intensive management
Lack of CAE testing and control program
Introduction of untested goats to negative herd
home_care: Provide soft, deep bedding for animals with arthritis
Keep feed and water accessible without need to stretch or climb
Provide high-quality nutrition with adequate vitamins and minerals
Shorten hooves regularly to improve mobility
Separate CAE-positive from CAE-negative animals
warning_signs: Complete inability to stand from arthritis
Rapid weight loss despite adequate feeding
Progressive hind limb paralysis in kids
Hard udder with very low milk production
Recurrent pneumonia
when_to_visit_vet: When arthritis or neurological signs first appear to confirm diagnosis and plan management. Annual testing of all goats is recommended for control programs.
prevention: Test and cull programs: remove seropositive animals from herd
Never feed unpasteurized pooled colostrum or milk to kids
Pasteurize all goat milk (133F for 30 minutes)
Raise kids separated from does after colostrum
Annual herd testing for CAE antibodies
affected_age: Kids 2-6 months (neurological form); adults 2-6 years (arthritis form)
recovery_time: Lifelong infection with progressive disease; no cure; management and prevention are key
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Caprine Arthritis Encephalitis
American Association of Small Ruminant Practitioners: CAE guidelines
Journal of Veterinary Internal Medicine: CAEV epidemiology

description: A toxemic disease caused by Clostridium perfringens type D, producing epsilon toxin in the intestine. The disease is triggered by sudden changes to high-carbohydrate diet, causing high mortality, often before clinical signs are observed.
common_causes: Clostridium perfringens type D bacterial overgrowth
Sudden access to high-grain or concentrate diet
Overeating of grain, bread, or lush pasture
Dietary change from forage to concentrate
Inadequate transition period during diet changes
symptoms: Sudden death in the best-conditioned kids or adults
Neurological signs: staggering, head pressing, convulsions
risk_factors: Kids and lambs on high-concentrate finishing diets
Access to grain bins, feeders, or spilled concentrate
Sudden introduction to lush legume pasture
Inadequate gradual transition to new feed
home_care: Immediate veterinary attention required
Remove all grain and concentrate feed immediately
Offer good-quality hay only
Provide clean fresh water
Reduce stress: quiet, dark environment
warning_signs: Sudden death of well-conditioned animals
Abdominal pain with bloating in a goat on high grain
Neurological signs: circling, blindness, head pressing
Severe diarrhea hours before death
when_to_visit_vet: Any suspicion of enterotoxemia is an emergency. Do not wait for symptoms to progress.
prevention: Vaccination with Clostridium perfringens type C and D toxoid
Prime: 2 doses 4 weeks apart; annual booster
Gradual diet changes over 7-14 days
Limit grain intake and provide adequate roughage
Divide grain into multiple small meals
recommended_tests: History and clinical signs strongly suggestive
Urine glucose test (high glucose in enterotoxemia)
PCR or ELISA for epsilon toxin in intestinal contents
affected_age: Most common in fast-growing kids and lambs 4-12 weeks; adults also susceptible
recovery_time: Peracute death within hours; acute cases 3-7 days with intensive treatment
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Enterotoxemia in Goats
American Association of Small Ruminant Practitioners: clostridial disease prevention
Journal of Veterinary Diagnostic Investigation: epsilon toxin detection

description: An intestinal parasitic disease caused primarily by Eimeria species (E. ninakohlyakimovae, E. arloingi). It is a major cause of diarrhea and poor growth in young kids, especially under conditions of stress, overcrowding, and poor sanitation.
common_causes: Ingestion of sporulated oocysts from contaminated environment
symptoms: Watery to mucoid diarrhea, sometimes with blood
risk_factors: Poor hygiene and infrequent bedding changes
home_care: Clean and dry living area with fresh bedding daily
Raise feed and water troughs to avoid contamination
Provide clean fresh water at all times
Offer highly palatable, nutritious feed
Isolate affected kids from healthy ones
warning_signs: Bloody diarrhea with straining
Severe dehydration with sunken eyes and skin tenting
Complete loss of appetite
Weight loss and poor body condition
Death in severe outbreaks
when_to_visit_vet: If diarrhea persists beyond 48 hours despite supportive care, if blood is present, or if multiple kids are affected simultaneously.
prevention: Good sanitation: clean, dry bedding regularly changed
Age-segregated housing
Avoid overcrowding and overstocking
Raised feed bunks and waterers
Rotational grazing of pastures
Coccidiostat feed additives for high-risk periods
recommended_tests: McMaster counting chamber for quantitative assessment
Necropsy with intestinal histopathology in fatal cases
affected_age: Primarily kids 3-12 weeks; adults develop immunity but can be carriers
recovery_time: 7-14 days with treatment; chronic cases may have permanent growth impairment
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Coccidiosis in Small Ruminants
American Association of Small Ruminant Practitioners: coccidiosis control
Veterinary Parasitology: Eimeria species in goats

description: Inflammation of the lungs in goats caused by bacterial, viral, or parasitic agents. The most common bacterial cause is Mannheimia haemolytica, while the lungworm Muellerius capillaris is a frequent parasitic cause.
symptoms: Loss of appetite and weight loss
Depression and isolation from the herd
home_care: Provide warm, dry, well-ventilated shelter
Ensure clean bedding free of dust and mold
Offer soft, palatable feed to encourage eating
Keep water source clean and accessible
Reduce stress and avoid handling sick animals
Separate affected from healthy goats
warning_signs: Severe difficulty breathing with extended neck and mouth open
Complete refusal to eat
High fever persisting beyond 48 hours
Blue-tinged mucous membranes (cyanosis)
Collapse or inability to stand
when_to_visit_vet: Any goat with fever, cough, and reduced appetite should be evaluated within 24 hours. Respiratory distress with mouth breathing is an emergency.
prevention: Good ventilation and dust-free housing
Avoid overcrowding
Vaccination against respiratory pathogens when available
Regular anthelmintic treatment for lungworm control
Adequate colostrum intake for kids
Proper nutrition with adequate selenium and vitamin E
recommended_tests: Deep nasal swab culture or PCR
Baermann technique for lungworm larvae in feces
affected_age: All ages; kids and elderly goats most severely affected
recovery_time: 2-4 weeks with appropriate treatment; chronic cases may have permanent lung damage
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Pneumonia in Sheep and Goats
Small Ruminant Research: caprine respiratory disease
Veterinary Clinics of North America: small ruminant respiratory disease

description: An insect-transmitted viral disease of sheep and goats caused by bluetongue virus (BTV, Reoviridae). While goats often show milder or subclinical signs compared to sheep, the disease can still cause significant production losses.
common_causes: Bites from infected Culicoides midges (biting midges)
Seasonal spread: late summer and autumn
symptoms: Facial edema: swelling of lips, face, and eyelids
Hyperemia and congestion of oral and nasal mucosa
Ulceration and necrosis of the tongue and dental pad
Abortions and stillbirths in pregnant does
risk_factors: Season with high Culicoides midge population
Low-lying, wet areas with midge breeding habitat
home_care: Provide shade and shelter from sun and midges
Offer soft palatable feed for animals with oral pain
Keep shallow water source accessible
House animals indoors during peak midge activity (dusk to dawn)
Provide soft bedding for lame animals
warning_signs: Blue-black tongue protruding from mouth
Severe respiratory distress from pharyngeal edema
Complete refusal to eat or drink due to oral pain
Death within 6-8 days of onset
when_to_visit_vet: Any goat with unexplained fever, oral lesions, and lameness should be evaluated immediately. BT is a notifiable disease in most countries.
prevention: Vaccination with attenuated live or inactivated BTV vaccine
Culicoides control: insecticide treatment of animals and housing
Avoid grazing near midge breeding sites at peak activity times
Housing animals at night during midge season
Quarantine of imported animals
recommended_tests: Virus isolation from blood or tissues
affected_age: All ages; clinical signs more common in young goats
recovery_time: 2-4 weeks; chronic cases may have persistent lameness
references: WOAH: Bluetongue technical guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Bluetongue in Sheep and Goats
Veterinary Research: BTV pathogenesis

description: A chronic, incurable enteritis of sheep caused by Mycobacterium avium subspecies paratuberculosis (MAP). The disease has a long incubation period (1-5 years) and is characterized by progressive wasting, persistent diarrhea, and death.
common_causes: Ingestion of MAP bacteria from contaminated pasture or feed
Contaminated colostrum or milk in lambs
symptoms: Chronic progressive weight loss despite normal appetite
Submandibular edema (bottle jaw) from protein loss
Exercise intolerance and lagging behind flock
Death from cachexia 2-6 months after clinical onset
risk_factors: Herd with previous Johne's disease history
Introduction of untested sheep from infected flocks
Young animals most susceptible to infection
home_care: Provide high-quality protein-rich feed to slow progression
Keep feed and water sources clean and elevated
Provide sheltered area away from weather
Separate clinically affected sheep from healthy flock
Remove manure from feeding areas regularly
warning_signs: Profuse diarrhea not responsive to treatment
Submandibular edema (bottle jaw)
Extreme emaciation despite eating
Weakness with inability to walk
Blood in stool or dark tarry feces
when_to_visit_vet: Any sheep with chronic weight loss and diarrhea should be tested. There is no cure, but early diagnosis allows management decisions to protect the flock.
prevention: Test and cull: serological and fecal testing
Raising lambs on clean pasture away from adult sheep
Avoid pooling colostrum from multiple ewes
Clean birthing areas
Fence off wet, marshy areas
Do not introduce untested sheep
recommended_tests: Fecal culture for MAP (gold standard, slow)
PCR for MAP DNA in feces
Necropsy with acid-fast staining of intestinal tissues
affected_age: Clinical disease typically in adult sheep 2-6 years; infection occurs in first few months of life
recovery_time: No recovery; progressive fatal disease. Incubation period 1-5 years, clinical course 2-6 months to death.
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Paratuberculosis (Johne's Disease)
WOAH: Paratuberculosis
Journal of Veterinary Diagnostic Investigation: MAP detection in sheep

description: A highly contagious bacterial infection of the hoof causing severe lameness and economic loss in sheep flocks. It is primarily caused by Dichelobacter nodosus in association with Fusobacterium necrophorum.
common_causes: Prolonged exposure to wet, muddy conditions
Contaminated pasture with infected hoof material
Introduction of infected sheep into clean flock
Poor hoof conformation and overgrown hooves
symptoms: Lameness ranging from mild to severe
Holding foot off the ground when standing
Under-running and separation of the hoof horn
Foul, distinctive odor from affected hoof
Swelling and redness of the coronary band
Undermining of the sole and heel
Weight loss in chronically affected sheep
risk_factors: Introduction of untested or affected sheep
home_care: Hoof trimming to remove loose, under-run horn
Foot bathing in formalin 2% or zinc sulfate 10%
Move sheep to dry pasture or housing
Spread lime at gateways and high-traffic areas
Isolate affected sheep during treatment
warning_signs: Severe lameness with inability to bear weight
Extensive under-running of hoof horn extending into sensitive tissue
Foul purulent discharge from hoof
Spread to multiple feet on same animal
Widespread flock involvement
when_to_visit_vet: If home foot bathing and trimming do not improve lameness within 7 days, or if the infection is spreading rapidly through the flock.
prevention: Quarantine new sheep for 4 weeks and inspect hooves
Regular hoof trimming every 3-6 months
Foot bathing after trimming and monthly in wet seasons
Culling of chronically infected and non-responding sheep
Maintain dry environment: improve drainage, rotate pastures
Avoid overstocking on wet pastures
recommended_tests: Hoof swab for culture or PCR for D. nodosus
Virulence testing of D. nodosus isolates
affected_age: All ages; more severe in adult sheep due to chronicity
breed_risk: Merino (more susceptible due to poorer hoof conformation)
recovery_time: 2-6 weeks with aggressive treatment; chronic cases may never fully recover
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Foot Rot in Sheep
Small Ruminant Research: ovine foot rot control

description: A metabolic disorder occurring in the last 4-6 weeks of pregnancy, primarily in ewes carrying multiple fetuses. Results from a negative energy balance when the energy demands of rapidly growing fetuses exceed dietary intake.
common_causes: Negative energy balance in late pregnancy
Inadequate feed intake due to competition or poor feed quality
Sudden weather stress or feed change
symptoms: Isolation from the flock and depression
Loss of appetite progressing to complete anorexia
Dullness with lowered head and closed eyes
Neurological signs: circling, head pressing, blindness
risk_factors: Underconditioned ewes with poor body reserves
Short breeding season with uniform lambing
Poor quality pasture or silage in late pregnancy
Late winter lambing with limited forage
home_care: Immediate veterinary attention required
Provide high-energy feed: good hay, molasses, grain
Offer palatable feed within easy reach without competition
Drench with propylene glycol 60-100ml twice daily as directed
Keep warm, dry, and comfortable with deep bedding
warning_signs: Complete anorexia for more than 24 hours
Recumbency with inability to stand
Neurological signs: seizures, blindness, head pressing
Fruity acetone breath
Rapid deterioration with progression to coma
when_to_visit_vet: Immediately at first signs of reduced appetite or isolation. Once recumbency occurs, prognosis is poor even with intensive treatment.
prevention: Body condition score (BCS) management: aim for 3.0-3.5 at breeding
Avoid overconditioning or underconditioning ewes
Increase feed energy density in last 4-6 weeks of pregnancy
Separate thin and fat ewes for targeted feeding
Provide adequate feeder space to reduce competition
Ultrasound pregnancy scanning to identify multiple fetuses
recommended_tests: Blood calcium to rule out hypocalcemia
affected_age: Adult ewes in last 4-6 weeks of pregnancy
breed_risk: Prolific breeds (Finnish Landrace, Romanov, East Friesian) carrying multiple lambs
recovery_time: 3-7 days with aggressive treatment; advanced cases may not recover
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Pregnancy Toxemia in Sheep
American Association of Small Ruminant Practitioners: pregnancy toxemia guidelines
Sheep and Goat Research Journal: metabolic disorders in ewes

description: A chronic bacterial disease causing abscessation of superficial and internal lymph nodes. Caused by Corynebacterium pseudotuberculosis, the disease is highly prevalent in sheep flocks worldwide.
common_causes: Infection through skin wounds and shearing cuts
Direct contact with draining abscesses from infected sheep
symptoms: Enlarged superficial lymph nodes (most commonly prescapular, prefemoral)
Abscesses that enlarge slowly over weeks to months
Caseous: thick, greenish-yellow pus with layered appearance
Abscesses rupture and drain through the skin
Chronic weight loss when internal lymph nodes affected
Respiratory signs if mediastinal lymph nodes involved
home_care: Lance and drain accessible abscesses when mature (away from flock)
Collect and dispose of pus: do not contaminate the environment
Clean and disinfect lancing equipment between sheep
Apply fly repellent to draining wounds
Isolate affected sheep during abscess drainage
Shear infected sheep last to avoid spread
warning_signs: Multiple draining abscesses across the body
Chronic weight loss suggesting internal involvement
Respiratory distress from mediastinal abscesses
Spread to uninfected sheep at high rate
when_to_visit_vet: When first abscess is detected to confirm diagnosis and plan control. If multiple sheep are affected, develop a flock-level control program.
prevention: Vaccination (CLA toxoid vaccine available in some countries)
Quarantine new sheep for 30 days and inspect for abscesses
Disinfect shearing equipment between sheep
Shear affected sheep last
Cull chronically infected and recurring cases
Good hygiene: clean pens, reduce wound contamination
recommended_tests: Culture of pus: C. pseudotuberculosis isolation
affected_age: Primarily adult sheep (1+ years); rare in lambs
recovery_time: Chronic infection; individual abscesses heal in 2-4 weeks with drainage; internal abscesses persist
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Caseous Lymphadenitis
WOAH: CLA technical factsheet
Australian Sheep Veterinary Society: CLA control guidelines

description: An insect-transmitted viral disease of sheep caused by bluetongue virus (BTV, Reoviridae). It is characterized by fever, oral lesions, facial edema, and cyanosis of the tongue. The disease is seasonally transmitted by Culicoides midges.
common_causes: Bites from infected Culicoides midges (biting midges)
Seasonal spread: late summer and autumn
symptoms: Facial edema: swelling of lips, face, and eyelids
Hyperemia and congestion of oral and nasal mucosa
Excessive salivation with foaming at the mouth
Ulceration and necrosis of the tongue and dental pad
Abortions and stillbirths in pregnant ewes
risk_factors: Season with high Culicoides midge population
Low-lying, wet areas with midge breeding habitat
Proximity to livestock in neighboring farms
Imported sheep or germplasm from infected regions
home_care: Provide shade and shelter from sun and midges
Offer soft palatable feed for animals with oral pain
Keep shallow water source accessible for animals with mouth lesions
House animals indoors during peak midge activity (dusk to dawn)
Provide soft bedding for lame animals
warning_signs: Blue-black tongue protruding from mouth
Severe respiratory distress from pharyngeal edema
Complete refusal to eat or drink due to oral pain
Death within 6-8 days of onset
when_to_visit_vet: Any sheep with unexplained fever, oral lesions, and lameness should be evaluated immediately. BT is a notifiable disease in most countries.
prevention: Vaccination with attenuated live or inactivated BTV vaccine
Culicoides control: insecticide treatment of animals and housing
Avoid grazing near midge breeding sites at peak activity times
Housing animals at night during midge season
Quarantine of imported animals
Vector-proof housing in endemic areas
recommended_tests: Virus isolation from blood or tissues
affected_age: All ages; young lambs 3-12 months most severely affected
recovery_time: 2-4 weeks; chronic cases may have persistent lameness and wool loss
references: WOAH: Bluetongue technical guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Bluetongue in Sheep
Veterinary Research: BTV pathogenesis

description: A broad term for abdominal pain in horses, representing a clinical sign rather than a specific disease. Causes range from mild intestinal spasm to life-threatening conditions requiring surgical intervention. Colic is the leading cause of emergency veterinary calls in horses.
common_causes: Gas accumulation in the large intestine (gas colic)
Impaction of feed material in the large colon
Displacement of the large colon (nephrosplenic entrapment)
symptoms: Looking at the flank or kicking at the belly
Rolling or thrashing on the ground
Lying down frequently or stretched out posture
Elevated heart rate (over 60 beats per minute)
Reduced or absent gut sounds on auscultation
risk_factors: Dietary changes: sudden feed changes or poor quality hay
Inadequate water intake, especially in winter
Age (older horses more prone to lipoma strangulation)
home_care: Remove all feed and water immediately
Gentle hand-walking if the horse is not in severe distress
Do not allow the horse to roll repeatedly (may cause twisting)
Monitor heart rate, respiratory rate, and manure passage
Keep the horse in a safe, well-bedded area
Call a veterinarian immediately
warning_signs: Heart rate over 60 beats per minute
Severe unrelenting pain with sweating and rolling
No manure passage for more than 12 hours
Distended or tympanic abdomen
Lack of gut sounds on auscultation
Pale or dark red mucous membranes
Prolonged capillary refill time (over 3 seconds)
when_to_visit_vet: Any episode of colic lasting more than 30 minutes, or any severe colic signs (rolling, heart rate >60), requires immediate veterinary evaluation.
prevention: Consistent feeding schedule with high-quality forage
Limit grain to 0.5% of body weight per day
Provide ad libitum fresh, clean water
Regular dental care to ensure proper mastication
Consistent anthelmintic program targeted to fecal egg counts
Daily turnout or exercise to promote gut motility
Avoid sudden dietary changes
recommended_tests: Rectal palpation to assess intestinal position and contents
Abdominal ultrasound for fluid and wall thickness
Abdominocentesis (belly tap) for peritoneal fluid analysis
Complete blood count and blood chemistry
affected_age: All ages; specific causes vary by age
breed_risk: Miniature horses (higher risk of hyperlipemia colic)
recovery_time: Mild medical colic: 12-48 hours. Surgical colic: 3-12 months for full recovery.
references: American Association of Equine Practitioners (AAEP): Colic guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Colic in Horses
Equine Veterinary Journal: colic epidemiology

description: A highly contagious bacterial infection caused by Streptococcus equi subspecies equi, characterized by abscess formation in the lymph nodes of the head and neck. It is one of the most common infectious diseases of horses worldwide.
common_causes: Fomite transmission on grooming equipment, tack, and human hands
Aerosol transmission over short distances in barns
symptoms: Profuse thick yellow nasal discharge (initially clear, becoming purulent)
Enlarged, painful, and hot submandibular and retropharyngeal lymph nodes
Lymph node abscess formation, rupture, and purulent drainage
risk_factors: Young horses (weanlings and yearlings most susceptible)
Horses coming from sales or auctions
Recent travel to shows, events, or boarding facilities
Large breeding farms or training barns
Lack of prior exposure or vaccination
home_care: Immediate isolation of affected horses
Separate feeding and watering equipment
Warm compresses to encourage lymph node abscess maturation and drainage
Soft, palatable feed (soaked hay, mash) for horses with swallowing difficulty
Gently clean draining abscesses with antiseptic solution
warning_signs: Difficulty breathing due to enlarged lymph nodes obstructing airways
Inability to eat or drink due to pain or swelling
Spread of abscesses to other body parts (bastard strangles)
Persistent high fever over 105F not responding to NSAIDs
when_to_visit_vet: Any horse with fever, nasal discharge, and lymph node swelling should be tested. Respiratory difficulty from throat swelling is an emergency.
prevention: Vaccination with S. equi M-protein vaccine
Quarantine new horses for 3 weeks
Separate feeding and watering for each horse
No shared equipment between horses without disinfection
Temperature monitoring of all exposed horses
Biosecurity protocols: footbaths, hand washing between horses
recommended_tests: PCR on nasal swab or abscess pus
affected_age: Primarily young horses 1-5 years; all ages susceptible
recovery_time: 3-6 weeks for full recovery; lifelong carrier status possible in guttural pouches (10% of cases)
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Strangles in Horses
AAEP: Strangles guidelines
Equine Veterinary Journal: S. equi epidemiology and control

description: A debilitating condition characterized by inflammation and structural failure of the laminae connecting the hoof wall to the pedal bone. Causes severe pain and can lead to rotation or sinking of the pedal bone within the hoof capsule.
common_causes: Carbohydrate overload: excessive grain or lush pasture access
Supporting limb laminitis due to contralateral limb injury
Retained placenta or metritis in mares
symptoms: Weight shifting between feet in front (classic rocking back)
Reluctance to move on hard surfaces
Increased digital pulse in the fetlock region
Heat emanating from the hoof wall and sole
Pain on hoof tester pressure over the sole
Standing with hind limbs positioned well under the body
Lameness progressing from subtle to non-weight-bearing
Radiographic rotation of the pedal bone
risk_factors: Easy keeper breeds with efficient metabolism
Lush pasture access in spring and autumn
home_care: Immediately move the horse to a deep sand or soft bedding stall
Remove all grain and pasture access immediately
Provide only low-sugar grass hay
Cold therapy: standing in ice water (18C or cooler) for 48 hours
Limit movement: confine to a small deeply bedded stall
Do not force walking
warning_signs: Inability to stand or extreme reluctance to move
Palpable heat in the hooves with bounding digital pulses
Visible sinking or rotation of the pedal bone on radiographs
Cresty neck indicating metabolic syndrome
Refusal to lift the opposite front foot
when_to_visit_vet: Any lameness with increased digital pulses and hoof heat is a laminitis emergency. Immediate veterinary assessment improves the chance of successful recovery.
prevention: Maintain lean body condition (BCS 5-6/9)
Limit lush pasture access with grazing muzzles or restricted turnout
Use slow feeders to mimic natural grazing patterns
Low-sugar, low-starch diet: grass hay, ration balancer
Regular exercise for non-laminitic horses
Monitor and manage PPID and EMS with veterinary guidance
recommended_tests: Radiography: foot series for pedal bone position and rotation
Blood insulin, glucose, ACTH (for PPID diagnosis)
Obesity scoring and cresty neck scoring
affected_age: All ages; highest risk in middle-aged to older horses and ponies (10+ years)
recovery_time: Acute phase: 2-7 days intensive care. Chronic phase: months to lifelong management.
references: American Association of Equine Practitioners: Laminitis guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Laminitis in Horses
Equine Veterinary Journal: laminitis pathophysiology

description: A highly contagious viral respiratory disease of horses caused by influenza A virus subtypes H7N7 and H3N8. Spreads rapidly through respiratory aerosols in horse populations, especially in congregated settings.
common_causes: Equine influenza A virus (H3N8 currently endemic; H7N7 believed extinct)
Contaminated equipment, human hands, and vehicles
Airborne spread over several meters in stables
symptoms: Deep, dry hacking cough that persists for weeks
risk_factors: Horses attending shows, competitions, or race meetings
Horses in training barns with high turnover
Breeding farms with frequent visitor traffic
home_care: Immediate isolation for 14 days
Complete rest in a well-ventilated stall for 1 week per day of fever
Provide soft, palatable feed and fresh water
Use fans or open windows for ventilation (dust-free environment)
Monitor temperature twice daily
Strict biosecurity: separate tack, buckets, and handlers
warning_signs: Difficulty breathing with extended neck
Refusal to eat for more than 24 hours
High fever persisting beyond 4 days
Thick purulent nasal discharge suggesting bacterial pneumonia
Collapse or extreme lethargy
when_to_visit_vet: Any horse with fever and cough should be isolated and tested. If fever persists beyond 48 hours or if respiratory distress develops, call a veterinarian.
prevention: Vaccination with inactivated or modified-live equine influenza vaccine
Primary course: 2 doses 4-6 weeks apart
Boosters every 6-12 months depending on risk
Booster 2 weeks before planned exposure (shows, sales)
Quarantine new arrivals for 14 days
Restricting movement during known outbreaks
recommended_tests: ELISA serology (paired samples for rising titers)
Complete blood count and acute phase proteins
affected_age: All ages; most severe in young horses (weanlings to 4 years)
recovery_time: Acute illness 3-7 days; cough may persist 3-6 weeks; full recovery 3-4 weeks
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Equine Influenza
WOAH: Equine influenza
Equine Veterinary Journal: EI epidemiology and vaccination

description: A syndrome of ulceration and inflammation of the equine stomach lining, broadly classified into squamous (ESGD) and glandular (EGGD) forms. Extremely common in performance horses, affecting up to 90% of racehorses.
common_causes: Prolonged gastric acid exposure due to infrequent feeding
Intense exercise reducing gastric blood flow and increasing acid exposure
Stress of training, transport, and competition
Fasting or meal-feeding with long intervals between meals
symptoms: Poor appetite or selective eating (hay over grain)
Weight loss or failure to maintain weight
Mild intermittent colic, especially after eating
Poor performance and reluctance to work
Dull coat and poor body condition
risk_factors: Intermittent feeding with long periods with empty stomach
