High grain, low forage diet (less than 50% forage)
home_care: Provide free-choice forage (hay or pasture) 24 hours a day
Reduce or eliminate grain/concentrate feeding
Turn out to pasture as much as possible
Feed alfalfa hay to provide natural antacid (calcium and protein)
Divide concentrate ration into small frequent meals if necessary
warning_signs: Severe weight loss despite adequate feed
Repeated episodes of colic
Complete refusal to eat
Dull, depressed behavior persisting for days
when_to_visit_vet: Any performance horse showing poor appetite, colic after eating, or poor performance should be evaluated. Gastroscopy is the gold standard for diagnosis.
prevention: Provide free-choice forage (hay or pasture) at all times
Feed a minimum of 1.5% body weight as forage daily
Reduce or eliminate grain, use balanced ration balancers instead
Maximize pasture turnout (24/7 ideally)
Avoid unnecessary NSAID use
Alfalfa hay supplementation for its buffering capacity
affected_age: All ages; highest prevalence in performance horses 2-10 years
recovery_time: ESGD: 2-4 weeks with treatment. EGGD: variable, may require extended therapy.
references: Equine Gastric Ulcer Syndrome Consensus Statement (ACVIM)
MSD Veterinary Manual: Gastric Ulcers in Horses
Equine Veterinary Journal: EGUS prevalence and management

description: A toxemic neurological disease caused by Clostridium tetani, a soil-borne bacterium. Horses are the most susceptible domestic species to tetanus, with a mortality rate exceeding 50% even with intensive treatment.
common_causes: Clostridium tetani spore contamination of wounds
Surgical wounds, particularly umbilical hernia repairs
Retained placenta or postpartum infection in mares
symptoms: Stiffness progressing to rigidity of the head and neck (lockjaw)
Erect ears and flared nostrils due to facial muscle spasm
Prolapsed third eyelid when the head is raised
Stiff, stilted gait with difficulty turning
Exaggerated response to sudden noise or touch (hyperesthesia)
Sawhorse stance due to extensor rigidity
Sweating, increased heart rate and respiratory rate
Difficulty eating and drinking due to trismus
risk_factors: Deep puncture wounds contaminated with soil or manure
Wounds on the lower limbs or hoof
Surgical wounds without adequate tetanus prophylaxis
Foals born to unvaccinated mares (no passive immunity)
home_care: Immediate veterinary attention at first suspicion
Minimal stimulation: quiet, dark, padded stall
Soft deep bedding to prevent pressure sores
Assisted feeding with slings if recumbent
Keep fresh water within easy reach
warning_signs: Rapid progression of muscle stiffness within hours
Inability to open the mouth or eat
Difficulty breathing due to laryngeal or thoracic muscle spasm
Collapse and inability to stand
Seizures or severe hyperesthesia
when_to_visit_vet: At the first sign of stiffness or hypersensitivity. Every hour counts: early antitoxin administration significantly improves survival odds.
prevention: Core tetanus toxoid vaccination: primary course of 2 doses 4 weeks apart
Annual booster
Pregnant mares: booster 4-6 weeks before foaling
Tetanus antitoxin for unvaccinated horses with wounds
Thorough wound cleaning and debridement
Surgical asepsis for all procedures
affected_age: All ages; most severe in young horses with no vaccination history
recovery_time: Protracted: 4-8 weeks in surviving horses; mortality rate 50-75% despite treatment
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Tetanus in Horses
American Association of Equine Practitioners: Tetanus vaccination guidelines
Equine Veterinary Education: tetanus management

description: A highly contagious and fatal viral disease of rabbits caused by Rabbit Hemorrhagic Disease Virus (RHDV, a calicivirus). It causes acute hepatic necrosis and internal hemorrhaging with mortality rates exceeding 90% in naive populations.
common_causes: Rabbit Hemorrhagic Disease Virus (RHDV, genus Lagovirus)
RHDV2 (variant): affects both domestic and wild rabbits
Direct contact with infected rabbits or their excretions
symptoms: Sudden death without any preceding signs (peracute form)
risk_factors: Outdoor or partially outdoor rabbit housing
Introduction of new rabbits without quarantine
Areas with known RHDV outbreaks in wild rabbits
home_care: No home care possible; prognosis is grave
Immediate quarantine of all rabbits on the premises
Do not move rabbits on or off the premises
Contact a veterinarian for confirmation and reporting
Strict biosecurity: separate clothing and equipment
Bury or incinerate dead rabbits
warning_signs: Sudden death of multiple rabbits within days
Blood from nose or mouth in deceased rabbits
Acute lethargy with rapid deterioration in hours
Difficulty breathing in previously healthy rabbits
when_to_visit_vet: Any sudden illness or death in unvaccinated rabbits requires immediate veterinary evaluation. Report suspected RHD to animal health authorities.
prevention: Vaccination with RHDV/RHDV2 vaccine
Revaccination every 6-12 months depending on vaccine type
Indoor housing or insect-proof outdoor housing
Quarantine new rabbits for 14 days
Footbaths and hand hygiene between rabbit enclosures
Control flies and insects in rabbit housing
affected_age: Rabbits over 8 weeks; younger rabbits may be resistant to classical RHDV
recovery_time: Peracute death within 12-48 hours; rare survivors take 1-3 weeks to recover
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Rabbit Hemorrhagic Disease
WOAH: RHD technical card
Journal of Veterinary Diagnostic Investigation: RHDV2 emergence

description: A severe viral disease of rabbits caused by Myxoma virus (a poxvirus). Characterized by swelling of the eyelids, ears, and face, progressing to skin tumors (myxomas) and severe immunosuppression. Mortality approaches 100% in domestic rabbits.
common_causes: Transmission by biting insects: mosquitoes, fleas, flies
Mechanical transmission via fleas and ticks
symptoms: Swelling and edema of the eyelids (blepharoconjunctivitis)
Swollen, drooping ears with secondary infections
Multiple skin nodules (myxomas) on the body
Difficulty eating due to facial swelling
risk_factors: Outdoor rabbit housing with insect exposure
Living in areas with wild rabbit reservoirs
Lack of vaccination (in countries where available)
Introduction of rabbits from flea-endemic areas
home_care: Supportive care: warmth, soft food, assisted feeding
Gently clean eyes and nose with saline
Keep in a quiet, insect-proof indoor environment
Provide soft, palatable food if the rabbit is eating
Ensure fresh water is accessible
Insect control in the immediate environment
warning_signs: Blepharoconjunctivitis with inability to open eyes
Complete loss of appetite with facial swelling
Difficulty breathing from nasal obstruction
Neurological signs: seizures, head tilt
when_to_visit_vet: Any rabbit with eyelid swelling, skin nodules, and fever requires immediate veterinary assessment.
prevention: Vaccination (Shope fibroma virus vaccine) in endemic regions
Indoor housing with insect-proof screens
Mosquito and flea control on pet rabbits
Avoid contact with wild rabbit populations
Quarantine and insect-proofing of new rabbits
recommended_tests: Clinical diagnosis based on characteristic signs
PCR on skin lesions or swabs
affected_age: All ages; most severe in young and immunocompromised rabbits
breed_risk: Domestic European rabbits (Oryctolagus cuniculus) most susceptible
recovery_time: 10-20 days; mortality 99%+ in domestic rabbits without vaccination
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Myxomatosis in Rabbits
World Rabbit Science: myxomatosis epidemiology
Veterinary Record: myxomatosis in pet rabbits

description: A common bacterial disease of rabbits caused primarily by Pasteurella multocida. It manifests in numerous forms including respiratory, abscess, genital, and systemic disease.
common_causes: Pasteurella multocida (primary pathogen, several capsular serotypes)
Stress-induced proliferation of carrier state bacteria
Poor ventilation and high ammonia levels in housing
Immunosuppression from overcrowding or poor nutrition
Dental disease predisposing to nasal cavity infection
symptoms: Sneezing with clear to thick white nasal discharge
Mucopurulent discharge that mats fur on forepaws
Noisy breathing with rattling or snuffling sounds
Red and swollen conjunctiva with eye discharge
Subcutaneous abscesses (especially on face and neck)
risk_factors: High ammonia from poor litter management
Stress from transport, weaning, or showing
Age: older rabbits more susceptible to chronic forms
home_care: Gently clean nose and eyes with warm damp cloth daily
Provide dust-free hay and bedding (avoid shavings)
Ensure good ventilation without drafts
Offer soft, palatable food if the rabbit is not eating well
Reduce stress: quiet environment, routine handling
Keep the rabbit warm and comfortable
warning_signs: Open-mouth breathing with respiratory distress
Complete refusal to eat for more than 12 hours
Severe head tilt with inability to stand or eat
Rapidly growing abscesses on the face
Weight loss despite eating
when_to_visit_vet: Any rabbit with nasal discharge, sneezing, or eye discharge persisting beyond 48 hours should be examined.
prevention: Good ventilation and low ammonia in rabbit housing
Clean, dry bedding changed regularly
Reduce overcrowding and stress
Dust-free hay and pelleted feed
Quarantine new rabbits for 2 weeks
Regular dental checks to prevent malocclusion
recommended_tests: Radiography of skull for dental disease
affected_age: All ages; chronic form more common in adult rabbits over 2 years
breed_risk: Brachycephalic breeds (Netherland Dwarf, Lionhead) more prone to respiratory signs
recovery_time: 2-6 weeks with treatment; chronic cases require ongoing management
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Pasteurellosis in Rabbits
Journal of Exotic Pet Medicine: rabbit respiratory disease
Veterinary Clinics of North America: exotic pet medicine

description: A parasitic disease caused by Eimeria species, affecting either the liver (E. stiedae) or intestines (E. magna, E. media, E. irresidua). It is a major cause of morbidity and mortality in young rabbits, especially under poor sanitation.
common_causes: Eimeria magna, E. media, E. irresidua (intestinal forms)
Ingestion of sporulated oocysts from contaminated environment
symptoms: Hepatic form: enlarged liver, jaundice, weight loss
risk_factors: Stress from weaning and diet changes
home_care: Clean and dry housing with frequent bedding changes
Raise feed and water containers to avoid fecal contamination
Provide clean, fresh water at all times
Feed high-quality, palatable diet
Isolate affected rabbits
Separate age groups
warning_signs: Severe watery diarrhea with dehydration
Jaundice (yellowing of eyes, gums) in hepatic form
Complete loss of appetite
Severe weight loss and emaciation
Death within days in severe outbreaks
when_to_visit_vet: If diarrhea persists beyond 48 hours, if jaundice develops, or if multiple young rabbits are affected simultaneously.
prevention: Good sanitation: clean, dry bedding changed regularly
Elevated feed and water stations to prevent contamination
Age-segregated housing
Avoid overcrowding
Coccidiostat in feed for high-risk groups
Regular cleaning and disinfection of hutches
recommended_tests: McMaster counting chamber for quantitative assessment
Necropsy with liver and intestinal histopathology
affected_age: Primarily young rabbits 4-16 weeks; adults develop immunity
recovery_time: 7-14 days with appropriate treatment; chronic cases may have permanent liver damage
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Coccidiosis in Rabbits
Veterinary Parasitology: rabbit coccidiosis
World Rabbit Science: Eimeria species in rabbits

description: A highly contagious viral respiratory disease of birds caused by Influenza A virus. Highly pathogenic strains (H5N1, H7N9) cause severe systemic disease with high mortality. It is a significant zoonotic disease with pandemic potential.
common_causes: Influenza A virus (multiple subtypes: H5N1, H5N8, H7N9, H9N2)
Live bird markets with poor biosecurity
Fecal-oral transmission via contaminated feed and water
symptoms: Sudden death with high mortality (up to 100% in HPAI strains)
Severe respiratory distress: gasping, coughing, sneezing
Purplish discoloration of comb and wattles
Sharp drop in egg production (up to 90%)
Neurological signs: tremors, paralysis, twisted neck
Hemorrhages on legs and internal organs
risk_factors: Outdoor or free-range poultry with wild bird contact
Live bird markets and trading networks
Introduction of new birds without quarantine
Areas with ongoing HPAI outbreaks in wild birds
home_care: No home care possible: this is a notifiable disease
Immediately report suspected cases to veterinary authorities
Strict quarantine of affected premises
Do not move birds, eggs, or equipment off the premises
Human protective equipment for all handlers
Follow official depopulation and disinfection protocols
warning_signs: Sudden death of multiple birds within 24-48 hours
Respiratory distress with swollen head and comb discoloration
Severe drop in egg production with misshapen eggs
Neurological signs in multiple birds
Hemorrhages on legs or internal organs visible on post-mortem
when_to_visit_vet: Any suspicion of avian influenza is a veterinary emergency and must be reported to animal health authorities immediately. Do not wait for laboratory confirmation.
prevention: Strict biosecurity: dedicated clothing, footbaths, restricted access
Limit contact between domestic poultry and wild waterfowl
All-in-all-out management with thorough cleaning between flocks
Vaccination in endemic areas (requires government approval)
Regular surveillance testing in high-risk regions
Proper disposal of dead birds
recommended_tests: RT-PCR on tracheal or cloacal swabs
affected_age: All ages; severity varies by strain and bird species
breed_risk: Turkeys more susceptible to severe disease than chickens
recovery_time: Peracute death within 24-48 hours (HPAI); low pathogenicity strains: 1-3 weeks recovery
references: WOAH: Avian Influenza technical guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Avian Influenza
CDC: Avian Influenza transmission
FAO: AI prevention and control

description: A highly contagious viral disease affecting many bird species, caused by Newcastle disease virus (NDV, paramyxovirus). Virulent strains cause severe respiratory, nervous, and digestive disease with high mortality. It is a notifiable disease worldwide.
common_causes: Infected wild birds and waterfowl as reservoirs
Live bird markets and trade networks
symptoms: Sudden death with high mortality in unvaccinated flocks
Neurological signs: tremors, twisted neck, paralysis, circling
risk_factors: Introduction of new birds without quarantine
home_care: No home care possible: this is a notifiable disease
Immediately report suspected cases to veterinary authorities
Strict quarantine of affected premises
Do not move birds, eggs, or equipment off the premises
Human protective equipment for all handlers
Follow official control protocols including depopulation if required
warning_signs: Sudden death of multiple birds within 24-72 hours
Severe respiratory distress with neurological signs
Complete egg production drop across the flock
Twisted neck and paralysis in multiple birds
Greenish diarrhea with dehydration
when_to_visit_vet: Any suspicion of Newcastle disease requires immediate reporting to veterinary authorities. ND is a notifiable disease with mandatory control measures.
prevention: Vaccination with live attenuated or inactivated NDV vaccine
Strict biosecurity: dedicated clothing, footbaths, restricted access
All-in-all-out management with thorough cleaning and disinfection
Quarantine new birds for 30 days
Limit contact with wild birds
Regular serological monitoring of flock immunity
recommended_tests: RT-PCR on tracheal or cloacal swabs
Serology (HI test, ELISA) for antibody detection
affected_age: All ages; severity higher in young birds
recovery_time: 2-6 weeks depending on strain virulence; mortality 10-90% in unvaccinated flocks
references: WOAH: Newcastle Disease technical guidelines
MSD Veterinary Manual: Newcastle Disease
FAO: ND prevention and control

description: A highly contagious viral disease of young chickens caused by Infectious Bursal Disease Virus (IBDV, Birnaviridae). The virus destroys the bursa of Fabricius, the primary immune organ, causing immunosuppression and increased susceptibility to other diseases.
common_causes: Fomite spread via equipment, clothing, and vehicles
symptoms: Swollen and inflamed bursa of Fabricius (on post-mortem)
risk_factors: Lack of vaccination in parent flocks
Persistent virus in contaminated poultry houses
home_care: Provide warmth and deep, dry, clean litter
Ensure easy access to fresh water and feed
Reduce stress: avoid unnecessary handling
Isolate affected birds
Improve ventilation
Supportive care with electrolytes in water
warning_signs: Sudden onset of depression in 3-6 week old chicks
High morbidity affecting 80-100% of the flock
Severe immunosuppression leading to secondary infections
Pasty vent with watery diarrhea
Dehydration and death
when_to_visit_vet: Any sudden illness in young chickens with high morbidity requires veterinary evaluation. Diagnosis is important for preventing immunosuppression.
prevention: Vaccination of parent flocks to provide maternal antibodies
Vaccination of chicks at 2-4 weeks with intermediate or intermediate-plus vaccines
Strict biosecurity and disinfection
All-in-all-out management
Thorough cleaning and disinfection between flocks
Long downtime (at least 3 weeks) between flocks
recommended_tests: Post-mortem examination: bursal swelling and hemorrhage
affected_age: Primarily chickens 3-6 weeks of age; older birds may have subclinical infection
recovery_time: 5-14 days; mortality 0-30% depending on strain virulence and flock immunity
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Infectious Bursal Disease
WOAH: IBD technical card
Avian Pathology: IBDV control in poultry

description: A slow-spreading viral disease of birds caused by Avipoxvirus, characterized by wart-like skin lesions on unfeathered parts (cutaneous form) and/or diphtheritic lesions in the mouth and respiratory tract (diphtheritic form). It causes production losses rather than high mortality.
common_causes: Inhalation of aerosolized virus from scabs
symptoms: Cutaneous form: wart-like nodules on comb, wattles, eyelids, legs
Nodules that progress to scabs and heal within 2-4 weeks
Diphtheritic form: yellow-white plaques in mouth, pharynx, trachea
Difficulty breathing if lesions obstruct airways
High morbidity but typically low mortality
risk_factors: Free-range or outdoor poultry with insect exposure
High insect populations in poultry housing
home_care: No specific treatment; supportive care and hygiene
Remove scabs gently if they interfere with vision or eating
Apply iodine or antiseptic to skin lesions
Provide soft feed if mouth lesions are present
Ensure easy access to water
Control mosquitoes in and around poultry housing
warning_signs: Lesions obstructing vision, eating, or breathing
Secondary bacterial infections of lesions
Rapid spread through the flock in the diphtheritic form
Loss of appetite with refusal to eat or drink
Respiratory distress in diphtheritic form
when_to_visit_vet: If lesions are severe, if the diphtheritic form affects breathing, or if production losses are significant. Vaccination advice is needed for future flock protection.
prevention: Vaccination with fowl pox vaccine (live, attenuated)
Routine vaccination in endemic areas (wing web method)
Mosquito control: screens, insecticide spraying, remove standing water
Biosecurity: quarantine new birds, disinfect equipment
All-in-all-out management with thorough cleaning
affected_age: All ages; more severe in young birds and during mosquito season
recovery_time: Cutaneous form: 2-4 weeks. Diphtheritic form: 3-6 weeks for full recovery.
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Fowl Pox
Avian Diseases: fowl pox management
WOAH: Fowl Pox technical card

description: An intestinal parasitic disease of poultry caused by Eimeria species (E. tenella, E. necatrix, E. acervulina, E. maxima). It is one of the most economically significant diseases of poultry worldwide, causing poor growth, reduced feed conversion, and mortality.
common_causes: Ingestion of sporulated oocysts from contaminated litter
Poor litter management and high humidity
symptoms: Decreased feed intake and poor growth
risk_factors: Wet, caked litter with high moisture content
Poor litter management and infrequent cleaning
Stress from environmental changes or transport
Repeated use of same poultry house without thorough cleaning
home_care: Maintain dry, clean litter (moisture below 25%)
Ensure good ventilation to reduce litter moisture
Provide adequate feeder and waterer space to reduce competition
Remove wet spots and caked litter promptly
Reduce stress: maintain consistent environment
Consider coccidiostat in feed during high-risk periods
warning_signs: Bloody diarrhea in young chickens
Severe dehydration and weight loss
Pale comb and wattles indicating significant blood loss
High morbidity affecting multiple birds simultaneously
Sudden death in apparently healthy birds
when_to_visit_vet: If bloody diarrhea is observed, if feed conversion drops significantly, or if mortality increases. Diagnosis and sensitivity testing help guide control programs.
prevention: Good litter management: maintain dry, clean litter
Avoid overcrowding and ensure adequate feeder/waterer space
All-in-all-out management with thorough cleaning between flocks
Coccidiostat in starter and grower feeds
Rotational use of anticoccidials to prevent resistance
Vaccination with live oocyst vaccines where appropriate
recommended_tests: McMaster counting chamber for quantitative assessment
Post-mortem examination (intestinal lesions and cecal cores)
Lesion scoring at necropsy for severity assessment
affected_age: Primarily young chickens 3-6 weeks; adults develop immunity but are carriers
recovery_time: 5-14 days with treatment; chronic cases may have permanent growth impairment and reduced feed efficiency
references: MSD Veterinary Manual: Coccidiosis in Poultry
Avian Diseases: coccidiosis control
World Poultry Science: Eimeria species and pathogenesis
